chapter_summary:
Geobarometry and geothermometry are the methods by which petrologists and geophysicists estimate the pressure and temperature conditions experienced by rocks during their formation or metamorphism. Classical approaches rely on thermodynamic equilibria among coexisting mineral phases. Exchange thermometers exploit the temperature dependence of element partitioning (e.g., Fe-Mg exchange between garnet and clinopyroxene), while net-transfer barometers use reactions that produce or consume phases and therefore depend strongly on pressure through the volume change of reaction. These calibrations typically express ln(K_D) or ln(K_eq) as linear functions of P and T, so that independent constraints from a barometer and a thermometer can be solved simultaneously for P-T conditions.

Elastic geobarometry represents a more modern, complementary approach that does not rely on chemical equilibrium. When a mineral inclusion is trapped inside a host crystal at depth, the inclusion retains a residual pressure (P_inc) after exhumation because the host acts as a pressure vessel. The inclusion pressure that would be observed at ambient conditions depends on the contrast in elastic properties (equation of state) between the host and the inclusion. An isomeke is the locus of P-T points along which host and inclusion have the same volumetric strain, so neither exerts stress on the other. If the entrapment conditions lie on or near the isomeke that passes through the measured residual inclusion pressure at room temperature, the entrapment P-T can be reconstructed. The isomeke analysis requires accurate equations of state for both phases and careful treatment of the geometry of elastic relaxation.

Modern thermodynamic geobarometry unifies these approaches by using internally consistent thermodynamic databases (e.g., Holland and Powell) together with activity-composition models for solid solutions. Rather than relying on a single reaction, multi-equilibrium or Gibbs free energy minimization methods simultaneously satisfy all independent equilibria among the observed assemblage, yielding a best-fit P-T estimate with quantified uncertainty. Software packages such as THERMOCALC and Perple_X implement these methods. In the context of the ThermoElasticCalculator, a ClassicalGeobarometer module would implement standard exchange and net-transfer calibrations, while an IsomekeCalculator module would compute isomeke curves using the Mie-Gruneisen-Debye or Birch-Murnaghan equations of state for host-inclusion pairs.

Applications of these methods are wide-ranging. Mantle xenoliths brought to the surface by kimberlite eruptions preserve mineral assemblages (olivine, garnet, orthopyroxene, clinopyroxene) whose compositions record the P-T conditions of the lithospheric or asthenospheric mantle. Garnet-orthopyroxene barometry (Al-in-orthopyroxene) and two-pyroxene thermometry are workhorses for constructing mantle geotherms. Diamond inclusions, analyzed via elastic geobarometry (e.g., quartz-in-garnet or coesite-in-diamond systems), provide independent depth constraints that do not require chemical equilibrium and can be applied to single-phase inclusions. Combining classical and elastic methods strengthens confidence in P-T estimates and has revealed details of mantle thermal structure, slab recycling depths, and diamond formation conditions.
prerequisite_knowledge: Chemical thermodynamics: chemical potential, activity, equilibrium constant (Chapters 1 and 12)
Equation of state: Birch-Murnaghan and Mie-Gruneisen-Debye frameworks (Chapters 5-6)
Solid solution thermodynamics and activity-composition models
Elastic moduli and their pressure-temperature dependence (Chapter 4)
Mineralogy of the upper mantle: garnet, olivine, pyroxenes, spinel
Basic petrology: metamorphic facies, igneous crystallization, exhumation paths
Finite strain theory and volumetric strain calculations

key_equations.name: Classical Exchange Thermometer (general form)

variables.K_D: Distribution coefficient for element exchange (e.g., Fe-Mg) between two phases [dimensionless]
variables.\\Delta H^0: Standard enthalpy change of the exchange reaction [J/mol]
variables.\\Delta V^0: Standard volume change of the exchange reaction [cm^3/mol or J/(mol·bar)]
variables.\\Delta S^0: Standard entropy change of the exchange reaction [J/(mol·K)]
variables.P: Pressure [bar or GPa with appropriate unit conversion]

key_equations.interpretation: For exchange reactions where the volume change is small, the P*Delta_V term is negligible and ln(K_D) depends primarily on T, making the reaction a good thermometer. The slope of ln(K_D) vs 1/T gives Delta_H, and the intercept gives Delta_S. Common examples include Fe-Mg exchange between garnet and clinopyroxene (Ellis and Green, 1979) and garnet and biotite (Ferry and Spear, 1978).

key_equations.name: Net-Transfer Barometer (general form)

variables.\\Delta G^0(T): Standard Gibbs free energy change of the net-transfer reaction at temperature T [J/mol]
variables.K_{eq}: Equilibrium constant expressed in terms of activities of end-member components [dimensionless]
variables.\\Delta V^0: Volume change of the net-transfer reaction [J/(mol·bar)]

key_equations.interpretation: Net-transfer reactions produce or consume entire phases and typically have large volume changes, making them pressure-sensitive. Setting Delta_G = 0 at equilibrium and solving for P yields the barometer. The GASP barometer (grossular + 2 Al2SiO5 + quartz = 3 anorthite) is a classic example with a large positive Delta_V.

key_equations.name: Isomeke Condition

variables.V_\\text{inc}(P,T): Molar volume of the inclusion phase at pressure P and temperature T
variables.V_\\text{inc,0}: Reference molar volume of the inclusion phase
variables.V_\\text{host}(P,T): Molar volume of the host phase at pressure P and temperature T
variables.V_\\text{host,0}: Reference molar volume of the host phase

key_equations.interpretation: An isomeke is the curve in P-T space along which the fractional volume change (volumetric strain) of the host equals that of the inclusion. Along this curve, neither phase exerts differential stress on the other. The isomeke slope dP/dT = (alpha_inc - alpha_host) / (beta_inc - beta_host), where alpha is thermal expansivity and beta is compressibility. Entrapment conditions must lie on or near the isomeke passing through the measured residual inclusion pressure at room temperature.

key_equations.name: Isomeke Slope (Rosenfeld and Chase, 1961)
key_equations.interpretation: The isomeke slope in P-T space is governed by the difference in thermal expansivity divided by the difference in compressibility between the two phases. If the inclusion is more expansive and less compressible than the host (e.g., quartz in garnet), the isomeke has a positive slope. The slope varies along the curve because alpha and beta are themselves P-T dependent, requiring numerical integration using full equations of state.

key_equations.name: Elastic Relaxation Correction (non-ideal geometry)

variables.P_\\text{inc}^{\\text{meas}}: Measured residual inclusion pressure from Raman shift [GPa]

key_equations.interpretation: The simple isomeke model assumes the inclusion experiences only hydrostatic stress. In reality, the finite elastic contrast between host and inclusion means the host also deforms. This correction, derived from Eshelby's inclusion theory for a spherical inclusion in an infinite matrix, accounts for the elastic relaxation of the host. It is significant when K_inc differs substantially from K_host.

key_equations.name: Al-in-Orthopyroxene Barometer (Brey and Kohler, 1990)

variables.a_{\\text{Mg-Ts}}^{\\text{Opx}}: Activity of Mg-Tschermaks component in orthopyroxene
variables.a_{\\text{Py}}^{\\text{Gt}}: Activity of pyrope component in garnet
variables.\\Delta G^0(T): Standard Gibbs free energy of the reaction at T [J/mol]

key_equations.interpretation: This is one of the most widely used mantle barometers. The Al content of orthopyroxene coexisting with garnet is strongly pressure-dependent because Al preferentially enters the garnet structure at higher pressures. Combined with a two-pyroxene or garnet-clinopyroxene thermometer, it yields the P-T conditions of mantle xenolith equilibration.

physical_concepts.definition: A method of estimating temperature based on the partitioning of elements (typically Fe and Mg) between coexisting mineral phases at chemical equilibrium.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: At higher temperatures, entropy favors more random distributions of Fe and Mg between phases, so the distribution coefficient K_D approaches unity. At lower temperatures, enthalpic preferences drive elements into specific sites, creating stronger partitioning. Measuring K_D from mineral compositions thus records the equilibration temperature.
common_misconceptions: That K_D is independent of pressure -- in reality, there is a weak P dependence through the Delta_V term that must be accounted for at mantle pressures.
That measured compositions always reflect peak metamorphic conditions -- retrograde diffusion during cooling can reset exchange thermometers, especially for fast-diffusing elements.

physical_concepts.definition: A method of estimating pressure from reactions that produce or consume entire mineral phases, exploiting the large volume change associated with such reactions.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: When a reaction has a large Delta_V (because product and reactant assemblages differ significantly in density), Le Chatelier's principle dictates that pressure strongly controls which side of the reaction is stable. By measuring the compositions (activities) of the phases, one can calculate the equilibrium pressure.
common_misconceptions: That net-transfer reactions are purely pressure-dependent -- they also depend on temperature through Delta_G(T), so a thermometer is needed alongside.
That activity models for solid solutions are perfectly known -- uncertainties in activity-composition models are often the dominant source of error in barometric estimates.

physical_concepts.concept: Elastic Geobarometry and Residual Inclusion Pressure
physical_concepts.definition: A method of estimating entrapment pressure by measuring the residual stress preserved in a mineral inclusion enclosed within a host crystal, then using the equations of state of both phases to back-calculate the trapping conditions.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: When a crystal grows around an inclusion at depth, both are at the same P and T. During exhumation, the host and inclusion would shrink by different amounts if they were free. Since the inclusion is trapped, a differential stress develops: inclusions that are more compressible or less thermally expansive than the host end up under residual compression. This residual pressure, measurable by Raman spectroscopy, encodes information about the entrapment depth.
common_misconceptions: That the measured Raman shift directly gives the entrapment pressure -- the Raman shift gives the residual inclusion pressure at room conditions, which must be converted to entrapment P-T using the isomeke framework and elastic relaxation corrections.
That elastic geobarometry requires chemical equilibrium -- it is purely mechanical and works even for single-phase inclusions with no chemical exchange, which is one of its great advantages.

physical_concepts.definition: A curve in P-T space along which two materials undergo the same fractional volume change relative to a reference state, so that no differential stress develops between them.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Imagine two blocks of different material glued together. As P and T change, they try to expand or compress differently. Along the isomeke, their volumetric strains happen to match, so they remain stress-free. Away from the isomeke, one material is compressed relative to the other. For host-inclusion systems, the entrapment isomeke is the one that passes through the measured room-temperature residual pressure.
common_misconceptions: That isomekes are straight lines -- they are generally curved because thermal expansivity and compressibility are P-T dependent. Linear approximations fail significantly over large P-T ranges.

That only one isomeke exists for a given host-inclusion pair -- there is a family of isomekes, each corresponding to a different reference state (i.e., different entrapment P-T or different measured residual pressure).

physical_concepts.definition: The temperature-depth (T-P) profile of the lithospheric mantle reconstructed by applying geothermometers and geobarometers to suites of mantle xenoliths erupted by kimberlites or alkali basalts.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Each xenolith records the P-T conditions at its depth of origin. A collection of xenoliths from the same pipe samples a range of depths, and plotting their P-T estimates traces out the local geotherm. This geotherm can be compared with conductive models to infer lithospheric thickness, heat flow, and whether the mantle was thermally perturbed before eruption.
common_misconceptions: That all xenoliths from one pipe are in equilibrium with a single steady-state geotherm -- some may have been thermally disturbed by the eruption itself or by prior metasomatic events.
That the xenolith geotherm represents present-day conditions -- it represents conditions at the time of eruption, which may be hundreds of millions of years ago.

physical_concepts.definition: The temperature below which diffusion becomes too slow to maintain chemical equilibrium, so that mineral compositions become frozen in and record a specific P-T condition.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: As a rock cools, diffusion slows exponentially. At high temperatures, mineral compositions continuously re-equilibrate. Below the closure temperature, diffusion effectively stops and the composition is locked in. Different thermometers have different closure temperatures because they rely on elements with different diffusion rates. Fe-Mg exchange in garnet has a higher closure temperature than in biotite, so garnet records higher-T conditions.
common_misconceptions: That closure temperature is a sharp boundary -- it is a concept from Dodson's theory that represents an effective average over a cooling interval.
That all minerals in a rock close at the same temperature -- different phases and different elements have very different diffusivities, leading to different closure temperatures.

key_references.author: Ellis, D.J. and Green, D.H.
key_references.title: An experimental study of the effect of Ca upon garnet-clinopyroxene Fe-Mg exchange equilibria
key_references.why_essential: Foundational calibration of the garnet-clinopyroxene Fe-Mg exchange thermometer, one of the most widely used geothermometers for mantle and granulite rocks.

key_references.author: Brey, G.P. and Kohler, T.
key_references.title: Geothermobarometry in four-phase lherzolites II. New thermobarometers, and practical assessment of existing thermobarometers
key_references.why_essential: Comprehensive calibration and assessment of thermobarometers for garnet lherzolites, including the Al-in-orthopyroxene barometer that remains a standard for mantle studies.

key_references.author: Rosenfeld, J.L. and Chase, A.B.
key_references.title: Pressure and temperature of crystallization from elastic effects around solid inclusions in minerals
key_references.why_essential: Pioneering paper introducing the concept of using elastic strain around inclusions to determine P-T conditions of entrapment -- the foundation of elastic geobarometry.

key_references.author: Angel, R.J., Nimis, P., Mazzucchelli, M.L., Alvaro, M., and Nestola, F.
key_references.title: How large are departures from lithostatic pressure? Constraints from host-inclusion elasticity
key_references.why_essential: Key modern paper on elastic geobarometry that derives the isomeke framework rigorously, including elastic relaxation corrections, and provides the EosFit-Pinc software for practical calculations.

key_references.author: Holland, T.J.B. and Powell, R.
key_references.title: An improved and extended internally consistent thermodynamic dataset for phases of petrological interest, involving a new equation of state for solids
key_references.why_essential: The internally consistent thermodynamic dataset (ds62) that underpins modern multi-equilibrium thermobarometry in THERMOCALC and related software.

key_references.author: Nimis, P. and Taylor, W.R.
key_references.title: Single clinopyroxene thermobarometry for garnet peridotites. Part I. Calibration and testing of a Cr-in-Cpx barometer and an enstatite-in-Cpx thermometer
key_references.why_essential: Introduces single-mineral thermobarometry applicable to mantle xenoliths, especially useful when not all phases are available for conventional two-phase calibrations.

key_references.author: Mazzucchelli, M.L., Burnley, P., Angel, R.J., Morganti, S., Domeneghetti, M.C., Nestola, F., and Alvaro, M.
key_references.title: Elastic geothermobarometry: Corrections for the geometry of the host-inclusion system
key_references.why_essential: Provides shape-factor corrections for non-spherical inclusions in elastic geobarometry, extending the Eshelby-based framework beyond the spherical approximation.

worked_examples.problem_statement: A garnet lherzolite xenolith from a kimberlite contains garnet with pyrope content X_Py = 0.72, clinopyroxene with Fe/(Fe+Mg) = 0.10, and garnet with Fe/(Fe+Mg) = 0.15. Using the Ellis and Green (1979) garnet-clinopyroxene Fe-Mg exchange thermometer, estimate the equilibration temperature at an assumed pressure of 5 GPa.
worked_examples.solution_approach: The Ellis and Green (1979) calibration gives: T(K) = (3104 X_Ca + 3030 + 10.86 P(kbar)) / (ln(K_D) + 1.9034), where K_D = (Fe/Mg)_Gt / (Fe/Mg)_Cpx and X_Ca is the Ca/(Ca+Mg+Fe) in garnet. We compute K_D from the given Fe/(Fe+Mg) ratios, convert P = 5 GPa = 50 kbar, and solve for T.
worked_examples.interpretation: The estimated temperature of ~1640 K (1367 C) at 5 GPa is consistent with a cratonic mantle geotherm at ~150 km depth. This places the xenolith in the lithospheric mantle, likely near the base of the thermal boundary layer. The relatively high K_D (1.59) indicates a moderately high temperature, as Fe preferentially enters garnet over clinopyroxene with increasing T.

worked_examples.problem_statement: A quartz inclusion in a garnet host from a metamorphic rock shows a residual Raman shift corresponding to an inclusion pressure of P_inc = 0.8 GPa at room conditions (298 K, 0.1 MPa). Using the isomeke approach, estimate the entrapment pressure assuming an entrapment temperature of 800 C (1073 K). The EoS parameters are: quartz K_0 = 37.12 GPa, K' = 5.99, alpha_0 = 3.34e-5 /K; garnet (almandine) K_0 = 173.6 GPa, K' = 5.3, alpha_0 = 2.28e-5 /K.
worked_examples.solution_approach: The isomeke is traced from the room-condition anchor point (298 K, 0.8 GPa for the inclusion, 0 GPa for the host) up to the entrapment temperature. At each step, we integrate dP/dT = (alpha_inc - alpha_host)/(beta_inc - beta_host) where beta = 1/K_T. We use the linear approximation for a rough estimate: Delta_P_isomeke ~ [(alpha_qtz - alpha_grt) / (1/K_qtz - 1/K_grt)] * Delta_T.
worked_examples.interpretation: The linear isomeke approximation gives an entrapment pressure of ~1.2 GPa at 1073 K (800 C). A full nonlinear integration with P-T dependent alpha and K_T would refine this estimate. The positive isomeke slope arises because quartz has higher thermal expansivity and higher compressibility than garnet. The estimate of 1.2 GPa at 800 C corresponds to ~40 km depth, consistent with lower crustal or upper mantle metamorphic conditions. The elastic relaxation correction (Eshelby) would slightly increase this estimate because K_qtz << K_grt.

worked_examples.problem_statement: A suite of garnet peridotite xenoliths from a Kaapvaal craton kimberlite yields the following P-T estimates from the Brey and Kohler (1990) thermobarometer: (2.5 GPa, 850 C), (3.5 GPa, 950 C), (4.5 GPa, 1050 C), (5.5 GPa, 1250 C), (6.0 GPa, 1400 C). Construct the xenolith geotherm and assess whether it is consistent with a conductive or advective thermal regime.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Plot P (depth) versus T for the five xenoliths. Convert pressures to approximate depths using P = rho*g*z with an average mantle density of 3300 kg/m^3. Compare the shape of the geotherm with a conductive model (curved, concave upward) and the mantle adiabat (~0.3 K/km). Look for a kink where the geotherm steepens, indicating the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary.
worked_examples.interpretation: The shallow xenoliths (76-136 km) define a conductive geotherm with a gradient of ~3.3 K/km, consistent with a surface heat flow of ~40-45 mW/m^2 typical of Archean cratons. The steepening gradient below ~140 km signals the transition from the conductive lithosphere to the convecting asthenosphere. The deepest sample at 6 GPa/1400 C likely samples the rheological boundary layer. This geotherm is consistent with a ~150-170 km thick thermal lithosphere beneath the Kaapvaal craton, matching independent constraints from seismology and diamond stability fields.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch01: Thermodynamics of Solids at High Pressure
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The thermodynamic framework for computing Gibbs free energies, chemical potentials, and equilibrium constants at high P-T is essential for all classical thermobarometric calibrations.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch04: Elastic Moduli and Seismic Velocities
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Elastic geobarometry requires accurate knowledge of bulk moduli, shear moduli, and their P-T derivatives for both host and inclusion minerals.
The SLB equation of state provides the thermodynamically self-consistent framework for computing volumetric properties needed in isomeke calculations.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch06: Mie-Gruneisen Equation of State Solver
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The Mie-Gruneisen-Debye EoS solver provides V(P,T) and K_T(P,T) calculations needed for tracing isomeke curves in the IsomekeCalculator.
The mineral parameter database provides EoS parameters for garnet, olivine, pyroxenes, quartz, and other phases used in both classical and elastic geobarometry.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch12: Gibbs Free Energy and Phase Stability
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Net-transfer barometers and multi-equilibrium methods require computing Delta_G of reactions as functions of P and T.
Geobarometry results constrain where in the phase diagram a rock equilibrated, and phase boundaries (e.g., graphite-diamond, quartz-coesite) provide independent checks on barometric estimates.
Knowledge of mantle rock compositions (lherzolite, harzburgite, eclogite) and their mineral assemblages determines which thermobarometers are applicable.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch28: Nonlinear Least Squares and Parameter Fitting
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Modern multi-equilibrium thermobarometry uses least-squares or maximum likelihood methods to find the best-fit P-T from overdetermined systems of equilibria.

computational_methods:
The ClassicalGeobarometer module implements a library of published thermobarometric calibrations as analytical functions T(P, compositions) and P(T, compositions). Each calibration is stored as a set of coefficients and a functional form. Given mineral compositions (end-member activities computed from site occupancies using standard mixing models), the module evaluates the thermometer and barometer simultaneously. Since many barometers depend on T and thermometers depend weakly on P, an iterative scheme is used: (1) assume an initial P, compute T from the thermometer; (2) use this T to compute P from the barometer; (3) repeat until convergence (typically 3-5 iterations for tolerance < 1 K and 0.01 GPa). Convergence is monitored by the change in P and T between iterations.

The IsomekeCalculator module computes isomeke curves for arbitrary host-inclusion pairs. The algorithm proceeds as follows: (1) At the room-temperature anchor point, the inclusion has a measured residual pressure P_inc,meas (from Raman spectroscopy) while the host is at atmospheric pressure. (2) The isomeke is traced by numerical integration of dP/dT = (alpha_inc(P,T) - alpha_host(P,T)) / (beta_inc(P,T) - beta_host(P,T)), where alpha and beta are evaluated at the current P for the inclusion and at zero pressure for the host (or at the current hydrostatic pressure for the host in the corrected formulation). (3) A fourth-order Runge-Kutta integrator steps from 298 K to the target entrapment temperature in increments of 1-5 K. (4) The Eshelby elastic relaxation correction is applied: the effective inclusion pressure is corrected by a factor involving (K_inc - K_host)/(4*G_host). (5) The entrapment pressure is read from the isomeke at the entrapment temperature.

For multi-equilibrium thermobarometry, the computational approach minimizes the sum of squared residuals of independent equilibria. Given n independent reactions among the observed phases, each reaction provides a constraint Delta_G_i(P,T) = 0. The Jacobian matrix of partial derivatives (d(Delta_G_i)/dP, d(Delta_G_i)/dT) is computed analytically, and Newton-Raphson iteration converges on the P-T point that best satisfies all equilibria. Typical convergence requires 5-10 iterations with quadratic convergence near the solution.
open_questions: How accurately can elastic geobarometry account for non-hydrostatic stress states in anisotropic host-inclusion systems (e.g., quartz inclusions in elastically anisotropic hosts)?
What are the effects of plastic deformation and viscous relaxation of the host on residual inclusion pressures, especially for inclusions that spent extended time at high temperatures?
Can machine learning methods trained on large xenolith databases improve thermobarometric estimates beyond classical calibrations, and how should uncertainty be quantified?
How significant are the effects of minor and trace elements (Cr, Na, Ti) on activity-composition models used in standard thermobarometers, and do current models adequately capture non-ideal mixing?
What is the correct treatment of fluid inclusions versus solid inclusions in elastic geobarometry, particularly for multiphase inclusions that have undergone partial back-reaction?
How can elastic geobarometry be extended to high-pressure polymorphic inclusions (e.g., coesite that has partially reverted to quartz) to reliably extract entrapment conditions?
To what extent do different internally consistent thermodynamic databases (Holland-Powell vs. Stixrude-Lithgow-Bertelloni vs. Berman) yield systematically different P-T estimates for the same rock?
exercise_suggestions: Implement the Ellis and Green (1979) garnet-clinopyroxene Fe-Mg thermometer and compute equilibration temperatures for three hypothetical garnet-clinopyroxene pairs with K_D = 1.0, 2.0, and 4.0 at P = 3 GPa. Discuss how K_D relates to temperature.
Derive the isomeke slope equation dP/dT = (alpha_inc - alpha_host)/(beta_inc - beta_host) starting from the condition that the volumetric strain of the host equals that of the inclusion.

Using Birch-Murnaghan EoS parameters for quartz and almandine garnet, numerically integrate the isomeke from (298 K, 0.5 GPa inclusion pressure) to 1000 K. Compare the linear approximation with the full numerical result and discuss the magnitude of the nonlinear correction.

For a hypothetical mantle xenolith suite with five P-T estimates, construct a xenolith geotherm and compare it with a purely conductive geotherm for surface heat flow q_0 = 40 mW/m^2. At what depth do they diverge, and what does this imply about lithospheric thickness?

Compute the Eshelby elastic relaxation correction for a coesite inclusion in diamond (K_coesite = 100 GPa, K_diamond = 440 GPa, G_diamond = 535 GPa) with a measured residual pressure of 3.0 GPa. How large is the correction relative to the uncorrected value?

Write a program that iteratively solves a coupled thermometer-barometer system: use the two-pyroxene thermometer of Brey and Kohler (1990) together with the Al-in-orthopyroxene barometer. Test convergence starting from different initial P guesses.

Investigate the sensitivity of isomeke-derived entrapment pressures to uncertainties in EoS parameters. Vary K_0 of quartz by +/- 2 GPa and alpha_0 by +/- 10% and compute the resulting range of entrapment pressures for a fixed residual inclusion pressure of 0.7 GPa at 298 K.

Compare P-T estimates for a garnet peridotite using three different barometer-thermometer combinations (e.g., Brey-Kohler, Nimis-Taylor, Nickel-Green). Discuss the sources of discrepancy and which combination is most robust.

figure_suggestions.description: Isomeke diagram for the quartz-in-garnet system in P-T space
figure_suggestions.axes: X-axis: Temperature (K or C), Y-axis: Pressure (GPa)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot a family of isomeke curves for different residual inclusion pressures (0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0 GPa). Overlay the quartz-coesite phase boundary. Show how the entrapment P is read at a given entrapment T. Highlight the curvature of the isomekes compared to linear approximations.

figure_suggestions.description: Xenolith geotherm for the Kaapvaal craton
figure_suggestions.axes: X-axis: Temperature (C), Y-axis: Pressure (GPa) or Depth (km), inverted axis
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot individual xenolith P-T estimates as points with error bars. Overlay conductive geotherms for different surface heat flows (35, 40, 45 mW/m^2). Show the mantle adiabat (potential temperature 1300 C). Mark the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary where the geotherm intersects the adiabat. Include the graphite-diamond transition for reference.

figure_suggestions.axes: No numerical axes; conceptual cross-section diagram
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show a spherical inclusion (e.g., quartz) inside a host crystal (e.g., garnet). Illustrate with arrows the residual compressive stress on the inclusion and the tensile stress in the host. Include inset showing the free expansion of the two phases at room conditions versus the constrained state. Label P_inc, P_host, and the elastic mismatch.

figure_suggestions.description: Sensitivity of the garnet-clinopyroxene thermometer to K_D
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot calibration lines for different pressures (1, 3, 5, 7 GPa). Show typical K_D ranges for granulites, eclogites, and peridotites in different shaded regions. Indicate the direction of retrograde re-equilibration with an arrow.

figure_suggestions.description: Comparison of classical and elastic geobarometry results
figure_suggestions.axes: X-axis: P from classical barometry (GPa), Y-axis: P from elastic geobarometry (GPa)
figure_suggestions.key_features: Plot a 1:1 line for perfect agreement. Show hypothetical data points for various metamorphic and mantle samples with error ellipses. Highlight systematic offsets where the two methods may disagree, e.g., due to retrograde re-equilibration affecting classical barometry but not elastic barometry.

figure_suggestions.description: Flow chart of the iterative thermobarometry algorithm
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show the iterative loop: initial P guess -> compute T from thermometer -> compute P from barometer -> check convergence -> output final P-T. Include decision boxes for convergence criteria and branches for different thermobarometer calibrations.

historical_context:
The foundations of geothermometry and geobarometry were laid in the mid-twentieth century as experimental petrology matured. In the 1960s and 1970s, experimentalists systematically calibrated mineral equilibria at controlled P-T conditions. Boyd (1973) established the garnet-orthopyroxene barometer for mantle rocks, and Wood and Banno (1973) and Wells (1977) produced early two-pyroxene thermometers. The Ellis and Green (1979) garnet-clinopyroxene Fe-Mg exchange thermometer became a cornerstone of mantle petrology. Brey and Kohler (1990) provided a comprehensive reassessment of lherzolite thermobarometers that remains widely used.

The concept of elastic geobarometry originated with Rosenfeld and Chase (1961), who recognized that inclusions in minerals preserve residual stress related to entrapment conditions. However, the method saw limited use until advances in Raman spectroscopy in the 1990s and 2000s made routine measurement of inclusion pressures feasible. The modern renaissance of elastic geobarometry was driven by Angel et al. (2014, 2015), who formalized the isomeke framework, derived rigorous elastic relaxation corrections using Eshelby theory, and provided practical software (EosFit-Pinc) for the calculations. Kohn (2014) independently developed the quartz-in-garnet (QuiG) barometer, which has become a powerful tool for metamorphic petrology.

Meanwhile, multi-equilibrium thermobarometry advanced through the development of internally consistent thermodynamic databases. The Holland and Powell dataset, first published in 1985 and continuously updated (most recently as ds62 in 2011), enabled THERMOCALC to simultaneously satisfy all equilibria among observed phases. Connolly's Perple_X (1990, updated continuously) provided an alternative approach based on Gibbs free energy minimization and pseudosection calculations. These computational tools transformed thermobarometry from a single-reaction exercise into a comprehensive thermodynamic analysis. The integration of classical chemical thermobarometry with modern elastic geobarometry and computational phase equilibria now provides a powerful, multi-method toolkit for constraining the P-T conditions of rocks from the crust to the deep mantle.
